schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take the twelve sons as the foundation of all the following genealogies and map the chapter's plan: Zerah's famous men, then Hezron's three branches (Ram to David, Caleb, Jerahmeel).
Gill notes the studied arrangement of the roll, the handmaids' sons ranged among the sons of the wives, Joseph and Benjamin set in the midst.
Henry reads the list as the tree of free grace: the covenant entailed on a blemished seed, Judah's tribe first and largest because Christ was to be its top branch.
Clarke refers the genealogy to its parallels in Genesis.

themes: free grace and a blemished line
Judah first for the Messiah's sake

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note the five sons follow Genesis 46:12, and that the Chronicler copies the verdict on Er verbatim from Genesis 38:7 because it suits the moral of his whole history.
Gill sets Judah first (with Kimchi) as the royal tribe, but chiefly because Jesse, David, and the Messiah sprang from him.
Wesley: Judah is placed first because the dominion of the birthright was given him (Gen. 49:8) and the Messiah came of his loins.

themes: Er slain in the sight of the LORD

schools.summary: Ellicott points to the full story in Genesis 38 and the spelling variants (Perez/Pharez, Zarah/Zara at Matt. 1:3); Keil and Delitzsch mark the two sons of Pharez as the branch that carries the line to David.
Gill states plainly the twins were born through incest, completing the five sons of Judah.
Ellicott ties the two sons to Genesis 46:12 and to the clans of the Hezronites and Hamulites registered in the census of Numbers 26:21.
Gill notes Pharez as one of the twins and names his two sons.

Ellicott identifies Zimri with Zabdi (letter-confusion, Josh. 7:1) and the four with the sages of 1 Kings 4:31 (Dara=Darda in many MSS), reading 'sons of Mahol' as 'sons of the sacred dance,' i.e. musicians adopted into Levitical guilds.

Wesley agrees the four are the wise men of 1 Kings 4:31 (whether by two names or a father/grandfather link); Clarke passes on the Targum that they were chief men on whom the spirit of prophecy rested.
Gill notes only that Zerah was the other twin of Judah.

debates.question: Are Zerah's sons here the same as the sages of 1 Kings 4:31 (and is Zimri the same as Zabdi)?

sides.position: the same men: Zimri=Zabdi by letter-confusion, and Ethan/Heman/Calcol/Dara(=Darda) are the sons of Mahol whom Solomon surpassed
or one bore two names, or an immediate father and grandfather are meant

outliers.note: Relays the Targum's claim that these five were chief men upon whom the spirit of prophecy rested.

themes: the wise men of Solomon's day
sons of Mahol as sacred musicians

schools.summary: Ellicott sets out the wordplay (Achar/'ocher/Achor, cf. Josh. 7:24-25) and warns against literal reading of 'son of'; Keil and Delitzsch note only his sin in the devoted spoil is recorded before the line returns to Hezron.
Gill (with the Targum and Jarchi) traces Carmi to Zimri/Zabdi and explains 'Achar' as a name of reproach for Achan.
Wesley: Achan is called Achar here with a slight variation for greater significance, since Achar means 'a troubler.'
Ellicott: nothing is known of this Azariah; the writer names only the famous members of the Zarhite branch (the sages of v.6, Achar of v.7).
Gill weighs why Zerah's line is cut short — the genealogy was lost, or (better) the kingdom did not come from Zerah, so the writer returns to Hezron.

themes: the line cut short at Zerah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch map the three branches (Ram to David, Caleb to Bezaleel, Jerahmeel), reordered by significance, and note Ram=Aram (Matt. 1:3); Ellicott gives the names' senses and reads Chelubai as a gentilic, so that clans, not just men, are in view.
Gill locates the birth of Hezron's sons, most likely in Egypt.

themes: three branches reordered for David's sake

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch align the line with Ruth 4:19, note Nahshon uniquely titled 'prince of Judah' (the Exodus leader), and press the chronological gaps — only three names for 430 years, four for 400 more — as proof that links are omitted.
Gill: Ram is Aram; the descent to Jesse matches the parallel lists, Nahshon alone here styled prince of Judah.

Gill canvasses why David is seventh here but Jesse had eight sons (1 Sam. 16:10): a son of another mother, one who died, or Elihu (1 Chr. 27:18) omitted; Jarchi says the writer, finding the pearl David, reckons not the eighth.
Wesley notes Eliab the firstborn is called Elihu in 1 Chr. 27:18, unless another man is meant and 'brother' is used loosely.

debates.question: Why is David the seventh son here when 1 Samuel 16 gives Jesse eight sons?

sides.position: an eighth son is omitted — by another mother, or dead before David's reign, or Elihu (1 Chr. 27:18), the writer naming only the pearl
the Syriac and Arabic insert him: 'Elihu the seventh, David the eighth'
the eighth was a grandson, Jonathan son of Shimea, not a son

themes: David the pearl of the line

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the sisters are named for their heroic sons — Zeruiah mother of Abishai, Joab, Asahel (father unnamed, the mother more famous), and Abigail (daughter of Nahash, half-sister, 2 Sam. 17:25) mother of Amasa.
Henry: David's three commanders were sons of one sister and Amasa of another, and three of the four went down slain though they were the terror of the mighty.
Gill notes only that Zeruiah and Abigail were sisters of David.

themes: the king's nephews as his warriors

schools.summary: Wesley harmonises 'Ishmeelite' (here) with 'Israelite' (2 Sam. 17:25): by birth or habitation an Ishmaelite, by profession a proselyte Israelite; Clarke relays the Targum's legend of the name Jether from his girding on the sword.
Gill identifies Amasa as Absalom's general, reconciled to David and slain by Joab (2 Sam. 20:10).
Keil and Delitzsch note Amasa, son of the half-sister Abigail by Jether the Ishmeelite, was thus of David's own kindred.

debates.question: Was Amasa's father an Ishmeelite (here) or an Israelite (2 Samuel 17:25)?

sides.position: both: an Ishmaelite by birth or dwelling, but by profession a proselyte Israelite

outliers.note: Relays the Targum's legend that 'Jether' was named from girding on his sword to aid David against Abner, who despised David's line for Ruth the Moabitess.

schools.summary: Ellicott finds the Hebrew corrupt and reads 'his wife, daughter of Jerioth,' since v.19 shows Azubah was a wife who died, not a daughter.
Henry sees a providence in Judah's full genealogies (for clearing Christ's line), notes Bezaleel the tabernacle-builder springs here, and draws comfort for the childless from the section.
Wesley makes Azubah the wife and Jerioth a barren concubine; Clarke relays the Targum on Azubah ('forsaken') spinning goats' hair for the tabernacle.
Gill distinguishes this Caleb, son of Hezron (=Chelubai), from Caleb son of Jephunneh.

debates.question: How are Azubah and Jerioth to be construed in the corrupt verse?

sides.position: read 'his wife, the daughter of Jerioth': Azubah daughter of Jerioth was Caleb's wife
Azubah the wife, Jerioth a (barren) concubine

outliers.note: Relays the Targum that Azubah ('forsaken') was barren and despised, then comforted and gifted, spinning goats' hair for the court of the tabernacle.

themes: Judah's genealogy and the line of Christ

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch trace Hur through Uri to Bezaleel the tabernacle-architect and note Ephrath=Ephratah (v.50; 4:4); Ellicott identifies Ephrath as the old name of Bethlehem (Micah 5:2).
Gill reports the Targum's identifying Ephrath with Miriam, but corrects it from Josephus: it was Hur, not Caleb, who married Miriam sister of Moses.

themes: Ephrath and the birth of Hur

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott identify this Bezaleel with the tabernacle-builder (the same names, father, grandfather, and tribe); Ellicott adds that Hur may be a Calebite clan at Bethlehem, easing the chronology.
Clarke takes him to be probably the famous artist of the tabernacle.
Gill doubts the identity, thinking this only a namesake of the tabernacle Bezaleel despite the matching fathers and tribe.

debates.question: Is this Bezaleel the artificer of the tabernacle?

sides.position: yes — the matching names, father, grandfather, and tribe prove it
no — only a namesake; the chronology forbids it

themes: Bezaleel of the house of Caleb

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the titles literally (Machir begot a son Gilead, cf. Num. 27:1); Ellicott suggests 'daughter of Machir' may be a clan, the verse recording an amalgamation of Hezronites with the Machirites of Gilead.
Gill gives Machir as son of Manasseh and Gilead his grandson, guesses the wife's name Abiah, and relays the Targum's 'enticed a virgin.'
Wesley notes Gilead was a man of noted valour, the champion of those parts.

debates.question: Is 'the daughter of Machir' a literal wife or a figure for a clan?

sides.position: a literal late marriage of Hezron at sixty, Machir begetting a son Gilead
a metaphor for a branch of Hezronites merging with the Machirite clan of Gilead

schools.summary: Ellicott gathers the Havoth-jair notices (Num. 32:40; Deut. 3:14), reading Jair as a group of clans in the 23 cities; Keil and Delitzsch note that Jair, a Judahite through Segub, is reckoned a Manassite through his mother.
Gill weighs whether Jair inherited the cities by his wife (Kimchi) or, more likely, took them by force from the former inhabitants.

Ellicott reverses the AV: Geshur and Aram (the Aramean kingdom, 2 Sam. 3:3) took the Havoth-jair and Kenath (cf. Num. 32:41) from the Jairites, and he harmonises the varying counts (Judg. 10:3; Josh. 13:30) as changing over time.
Gill follows the AV (Jair took Geshur and Aram) but cites the Targum's opposite sense, that the Geshurites took Jair's villages from his later descendants.
Wesley: the cities fell to Machir's sons and to his son-in-law Jair, reckoned a son for his affection and his casting in his lot with them.

debates.question: Did Jair take Geshur and Aram, or did Geshur and Aram take the cities from the Jairites?

sides.position: Jair was the conqueror (the Authorized reading)
Geshur and Aram seized the Havoth-jair and Kenath from Jair's line

themes: reckoned to the sons of Machir

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch keep 'Caleb-ephratah' as an (obscure) place-name near Bethlehem; Ellicott judges the text corrupt and follows the LXX to read 'Caleb went in to Ephrath his father's widow,' with Ashur='man of Hur.'
Gill takes Caleb-ephratah as Bethlehem, where Hezron died and his widow Abiah bore Ashur, father (prince) of Tekoa.

debates.question: How is 'in Caleb-ephratah' to be read?

sides.position: a place-name, Caleb-ephratah = Bethlehem, where Hezron died
a corruption; read (with the LXX) 'Caleb went in to Ephrath his father's widow, and she bare Ashur'

themes: Caleb as the house, not the man

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place the Jerahmeelites in the Negeb (1 Sam. 27:10) and read Ahijah, unjoined by the copula, as the mother's name (a dropped letter, 'from Ahijah'); Ellicott lays out the variants (LXX 'his brother,' Syriac 'their sister').
Gill notes Jerahmeel's firstborn was named Ram after his uncle (v.9).

debates.question: What is 'Ahijah' at the end of the list?

sides.position: the name of the first wife/mother ('from Ahijah'), a dropped letter restored
'his brother' (LXX) or 'their sister' Ozem (Syriac, Arabic)

themes: recovering the text from the versions

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch order the two branches (Onam's line vs Ram's three sons); Ellicott glosses Atarah as 'crown,' perhaps a city's fortifications (cf. the town Ataroth), the name shading toward a place.
Gill judges Atarah a true second wife rather than the harlot/concubine that Kimchi's 'another woman' might suggest.

themes: Onam and the line to Sheshan

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the firstborn Ram's three sons are named but not pursued; the record follows the younger Onam's line (vv.28-33) to Sheshan and his daughters.
Gill assigns Maaz, Jamin, and Eker to Ram by Jerahmeel's first wife.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the branching: Onam's sons Shammai and Jada, Shammai's sons Nadab (toward Sheshan and the heiress Ahlai, v.34) and Abishur.
Gill identifies these as the sons of Onam, Jerahmeel's son by his other wife Atarah.
Keil and Delitzsch place Abihail as wife of Abishur (son of Shammai), bearing Ahban and Molid, with whom this branch terminates.
Gill notes the name Abihail was borne also by a wife of Rehoboam (2 Chr. 11:18), though this is another woman.
Keil and Delitzsch carry the surviving line (Appaim, Ishi, Sheshan, Ahlai); Ellicott reads 'died without children' as a clan that failed to multiply and subdivide (cf. v.32).
Gill names Nadab's sons Seled and Appaim, Seled dying childless.

themes: childlessness or a clan not multiplying

schools.summary: Ellicott takes Ahlai as a clan-name, rejecting the older expedients, and links it to the crux of v.34 where Sheshan has no sons.
Gill explains the plural 'sons/children' as including the posterity though each man had but one son.
Wesley: 'sons' for one son is a manner of speech found even in profane authors.

debates.question: Is Ahlai a son, a daughter/heiress, or a clan?

sides.position: a clan-name
an individual heir, the plural covering a single descendant

themes: the plural for a single son
a house hanging on an only child

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the Jerahmeelite line closes with Jonathan's sons and the subscription (v.33), and report Bertheau's count of seventy descendants of Judah.
Gill names Jada's sons Jether and Jonathan, Jether dying childless like Seled (v.30).

Keil and Delitzsch mark the subscription closing Jerahmeel's line, then note the register doubles back to Sheshan, whose daughters and Egyptian servant (v.34) begin the one extended story of the section.
Gill notes Peleth and Zaza are mentioned nowhere else.

themes: houses that end and one that is preserved

schools.summary: Ellicott sets out the clash with v.31: either Ahlai was a daughter, or the Chronicler has preserved fragments of two independent accounts.
Gill reconciles it by making Ahlai the one daughter (plural for singular), or a son who died childless.
Clarke relays the Targum that Sheshan freed Jarha before giving him his daughter.

debates.question: How is 'Sheshan had no sons' reconciled with 'the children of Sheshan; Ahlai' (v.31)?

sides.position: Ahlai was a daughter (the plural for a singular)
the Chronicler preserved fragments of two independent accounts

schools.summary: Henry draws the lesson: the Egyptian servant must have been a worthy proselyte, and neither foreign birth nor servitude should bar the preferment of converts who do worthily.
Gill identifies the daughter as Ahlai, since Zabad is later called 'son of Ahlai' (1 Chr. 11:41), the line reckoned from the mother.
Wesley: Jarha was probably an eminent proselyte, else an Israelite would not have given him his only daughter.

themes: Ahlai and the line reckoned from the mother

schools.summary: Clarke insists this is Caleb son of Hezron, not son of Jephunneh (rejecting the 'grandson' theory), and relays the Targum's 'prince of the Ziphites' for 'father of Ziph.'
Gill identifies Caleb as Chelubai (v.18) and reckons these sons by another wife.
Keil and Delitzsch carry the line on (Shema to Raham, Rekem to Shammai) as the spreading of Caleb's house into Judean settlements, Hebron itself standing as a 'son.'
Gill identifies Tappuah (and Beth-tappuah) and Shema as towns of Judah named from these men.

themes: the clans of Caleb in their towns

schools.summary: Gill gives Jorkoam as a town (Hillerus) and cites Jarchi's rule that 'father' throughout these verses means the prince or chief of the city named.
Keil and Delitzsch set Raham and Shammai in the widening descent of Caleb's house across the Judean country.

themes: Jarchi's rule: 'father' = prince of a town
the clan widening across the land
obscure names kept in the register

schools.summary: Gill: Maon named a city of Judah, and 'father of Bethzur' means its founder or prince.
Keil and Delitzsch order the descent (Shammai, Maon, Beth-zur), clan and town descending together in the settlement of the south.

themes: the genealogy as a map of Judah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch treat Caleb's sons by his concubines and puzzle over the repeated Gazez — perhaps a grandson, an explanatory gloss, or a son named after an uncle.
Gill explains Ephah as a concubine (a secondary wife), noting Caleb kept concubines beside his one wife at a time.
Keil and Delitzsch note Jahdai appears abruptly with no genealogical connection, reject Hiller's guess that he is Moza, and mark the recurrence of names (Ephah, Shaaph) for different men.
Gill guesses Jahdai may be Moza under another name, a son of Moza, or another of Caleb's concubines.
Keil and Delitzsch note Maachah a common name (cf. 1 Chr. 3:2; 7:16), Sheber and Tirhanah unknown, and the masculine 'bare' (vs. the feminine at v.46) as the father coming to the writer's mind.
Gill notes Maachah as another (a third) concubine of Caleb.
Keil and Delitzsch locate Madmannah, Machbenah, and Gibea as towns of southern Judah (Josh. 15:31, 57), and note the list closes with a person, the daughter Achsah.
Wesley states plainly that Madmannah and divers following names are names of places in Judah.
Gill reads Shaaph as prince of Madmannah, a Judean town.

themes: father and daughter closing the clan

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the heading not as a new 'Caleb son of Hur' but (from v.19) as limiting the following lists to Caleb's descendants through his son Hur, firstborn of Ephratah.
Gill takes it as a third Caleb — a grandson of Caleb son of Hezron, named after him, the son of Hur by Ephratah.

debates.question: Does 'Caleb the son of Hur' name a third Caleb or the descendants of Caleb through Hur?

sides.position: a third Caleb, grandson of the Hezronite Caleb, named after him
no new Caleb; the clause limits the lists to Caleb's line through his son Hur (v.19)

themes: a third Caleb or the line of Hur

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place Salma (father of Bethlehem) and Hareph (father of Beth-gader) in the descent of Hur's line.
Gill distinguishes this Salma, prince of Bethlehem, from the Salma of the royal line (son of Nahshon, v.11).
Wesley reads 'father of Bethlehem' as the inhabitants of Bethlehem, Salma the clan-head.

themes: the town of the coming King

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take 'Haroeh and half the Manahethites' as proper names (Haroeh = Reaiah, cf. 1 Chr. 4:2), a clan split between two houses (cf. v.54).
Gill argues the phrasing proves Kirjath-jearim is a place, not a man, and Shobal its prince.

outliers.note: Relays the Targum's expansion that Shobal's 'sons' were disciples and priests entitled to half the oblations.

themes: 'father' proving a place, not a man
a town and its ruling clan

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch link the Ithrites to David's heroes Ira and Gareb (2 Sam. 23:38) and derive from these families the men of Zorah (Samson's town) and Eshtaol (Josh. 15:33).
Gill: these families dwelt at Kirjath-jearim, of which Shobal was prince, and sprang from him.

outliers.note: Relays the Targum that these clans were the children of Moses by Zipporah, and their offspring the disciples of the prophets at Zorah and Eshtaol.

themes: small clans opening on the histories

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch identify the Netophathites (near Bethlehem, and famed, 2 Sam. 23:28; Ezra 2:22), Ataroth-beth-Joab as a place, the Manahethites (cf. v.52), and the Zorites as men of Zorah.
Gill takes Bethlehem and Netophah for their inhabitants, of whom Salma was prince.

outliers.note: Relays the Targum's legend that the Netophathites removed Jeroboam's guards to carry first-fruits secretly to Jerusalem, and came of the line of Joab.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place Jabez near Zorah, read the three names as clans (Tira, Shimea, Suchah) against the Vulgate's appellative rendering, and note the scribes were Kenites of the house of Rechab.

Wesley reckons the scribes as Kenites dwelling among Judah, incorporated by marriage; Clarke relays the Targum's legend and gives the capstone: 'in this chapter many names of cities are given as the names of men.'
Gill: families of scribes dwelt at Jabez, a Judean city (cf. 1 Chr. 4:9), where learned men lived.

debates.question: Are 'Tirathites, Shimeathites, Suchathites' proper clan-names or appellatives?

sides.position: proper names, descendants of Tira, Shimea, and Suchah
appellatives ('singing, sounding, dwelling in tents'), as the Vulgate rendered
holders: the Vulgate (as reported by Keil and Delitzsch)

outliers.note: Relays the Targum that the scribes were Rechabites of the family of Moses whose righteousness profited them more than chariots and horses; and observes that many names of cities are here given as names of men.

themes: the Kenites and the house of Rechab

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take the completed twelve as the compressed foundation for the tribal genealogies, from which the record at once narrows to Judah.
Gill notes the Chronicler's own ordering, weaving the handmaids' sons among the wives' sons and setting Rachel's children in the midst.

themes: one Israel, the history through Judah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch align the link with Ruth 4:20 (Salma=Salmah/Salmon), noting the same telescoping flagged at v.10, and centre the verse on Boaz, the Bethlehemite ancestor of David.

themes: the parallel with the book of Ruth
a Gentile in the royal line

schools.summary: Wesley: David is seventh here though Jesse had eight sons (1 Sam. 16:10), one having probably died soon after, so the register counts the surviving heads.
Gill's fuller reckoning at v.13 stands behind the note; the discrepancy over the eighth son touches nothing essential.

themes: the register of the living heads
the goal of the genealogy reached
